title: NIST AI 600-1: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative Artificial Intelligence Profile
analysis: The foundational companion to NIST AI RMF 1.0, specifically adapted for generative AI. Translates the four core functions (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage) into concrete steps for GenAI. Covers hallucinations, data leakage, copyright, harmful bias, misinformation, and dual-use capabilities. The GIzn profile emphasises that AI systems are socio-technical — their risk properties emerge from the interaction of technical capability, deployment context, and human use. Notably, NIST distinguishes between 'high stakes' and 'lower stakes' AI applications, implying that trust mechanisms must be proportional. The framework is descriptive, not prescriptive — it provides a structural map rather than a compliance checklist. For practitioners, the key insight is that NIST AI RMF is a governance scaffold that different sectors must populate with sector-specific meaning.
relevance: Foundation for AI risk management — maps all major trust dimensions

title: EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689)
source: Official Journal of the European Union
analysis: The world's first comprehensive AI regulation, establishing a risk-tiered framework from minimal risk to prohibited applications. Prohibited AI practices include social scoring and real-time biometric surveillance with some exceptions. High-risk AI systems (Annex III) require conformity assessments, technical documentation, human oversight mechanisms, and registration in the EU database. The Act's emphasis on human oversight (Article 14) is particularly significant — it requires that high-risk AI systems be designed to allow effective human oversight, and that humans be able understand the system's outputs and make informed decisions. Article 14 explicitly acknowledges that oversight must be meaningful, not performative. The Act also introduces the concept of 'post-market monitoring' obligations for providers. The prohibited practices and high-risk categories represent a geopolitical benchmark that other jurisdictions will use for comparison. The Act's extraterritorial reach (applies to AI systems whose outputs affect EU persons) creates de facto global standards pressure.
relevance: Global benchmark regulation — defines the minimum bar for AI trust in affected organisations

title: Constitutive knowledge sources: an institutional approach to epistemic trust in opaque AI systems
analysis: This paper makes a crucial distinction that goes to the heart of first-principles trust theory. Gazit et al. argue that when AI systems operate as epistemic instruments — sources of knowledge rather than mere tools — the question of what grounds our trust becomes philosophically distinct from traditional tool trust. They introduce 'constitutive knowledge sources' as institutional anchors that provide warranted credibility to AI systems. The key insight is that institutional epistemic trust requires more than technical accuracy; it requires socially embedded warrant structures: professional certifications, regulatory approvals, peer validation, and institutional accountability chains. The paper critiques purely technical approaches to AI trust as insufficient — trust in AI as a knowledge source requires social institutions that can be held accountable. For financial services, this translates directly: an AI model's accuracy is necessary but not sufficient for warranted trust; there must be institutional structures that vouch for the model's epistemic validity in specific use contexts. This has profound implications for how we think about model validation, certification chains, and the accountability of AI-driven knowledge claims.
relevance: Philosophical foundation for why institutional trust structures are essential, not optional

title: Risk-Tiered Human Oversight of AI: Calibrating Trust and Reliance in the S-TIAS Framework
analysis: The S-TIAS (Socio-Technical Framework for Interactive AI Systems) framework offers a rigorous approach to calibrating human trust and reliance on AI systems across different risk tiers. Rather than treating all AI oversight as equivalent, S-TIAS proposes that the intensity and nature of human oversight should be proportional to the risk profile of the task and the AI system's demonstrated reliability in that context. The framework operationalises 'meaningful human oversight' by distinguishing between cognitive oversight (human reviews AI reasoning), managerial oversight (human approves AI actions), and procedural oversight (human monitors aggregate outcomes). McGrath et al. demonstrate through empirical studies that organisations implementing risk-tiered oversight achieve significantly better safety outcomes than those applying uniform oversight standards. Critically, the framework shows that for low-risk tasks, over-oversight generates its own risks — human complacency andautomation bias — while under-oversight in high-risk contexts enables catastrophic failures. The practical implication is that trust practitioners need sophisticated instruments for measuring task criticality and AI reliability in context, not binary human-in-the-loop flags.
relevance: Operationalises the 'meaningful' in meaningful human oversight — risk-tiered, not binary

title: Redefining the Standard of Human Oversight for AI Negligence
authors: Harvard Law School Journal of Law and Technology
analysis: This Harvard JOLT article addresses the liability dimension directly. It argues that the prevailing standard for AI negligence — what a reasonably careful human operator would have done — is becoming incoherent as AI systems make decisions that exceed human cognitive capacity to verify. The article traces how courts are beginning to shift toward what it calls 'structured discretion' standards: rather than asking whether a human should have caught the AI's error, courts increasingly ask whether the human oversight structure was appropriately designed for the AI's decision domain and confidence level. The article identifies three emerging liability paradigms: (1) the 'design defect' theory — liability for inadequate oversight architecture; (2) the 'post-market monitoring' theory — liability for failure to detect and respond to AI performance degradation; and (3) the 'epistemic overreach' theory — liability for deploying AI beyond its warranted confidence domain. For financial services practitioners, the critical insight is that organisations can no longer rely on 'human reviewed' as a complete liability shield; the structure and sophistication of that review is now legally scrutinised. This represents a fundamental shift in the consequence of trust — trust without appropriate warrant structures is now a source of liability, not protection.
relevance: Explains why liability frameworks must restructure — human review is no longer a sufficient shield

title: Attestation Across the AI Supply Chain
analysis: This analysis applies supply chain thinking to AI trust, arguing that AI systems are assembled from components — training data, pre-trained models, fine-tuning datasets, deployment infrastructure — each with distinct trust properties and degradation curves. The 'attestation' concept, borrowed from software supply chain security (SBOM, SLSA), proposes that each component in the AI supply chain should carry machine-readable evidence of its provenance, testing history, and known limitations. The analysis identifies a critical gap: most organisations treating AI as a monolithic 'vendor' have no visibility into the component-level trust properties of their AI systems, and no contractual or technical mechanism to verify attestations from upstream providers. The paper proposes a tiered attestation model: (1) foundational model attestation (what was the training data, what known failure modes exist); (2) fine-tuning attestation (what domain data was used, how does it modify base model behaviour); (3) deployment attestation (what guardrails were applied, what monitoring is active). This supply chain lens reveals that trust in an AI system is only as strong as the weakest attestation link — a critical insight for financial services where model provenance is often opaque.
relevance: Makes concrete how to implement trust provenance across the AI value chain

title: The Standard of Human Oversight for Algorithmic Trading: A Negligence Analysis
analysis: An extended version of the negligence analysis focused on algorithmic trading in financial markets. It argues that high-frequency trading algorithms present a test case for the limits of human oversight: no human can meaningfully review thousands of trades per second in real time. The article traces how courts and regulators are beginning to apply a 'design adequacy' standard — did the firm's oversight architecture, taken as a whole, provide reasonable assurance that the AI system would not cause foreseeable harm? This is structurally different from traditional negligence, which focuses on individual actions. The design adequacy standard focuses on systemic properties: whether the monitoring infrastructure, escalation mechanisms, circuit breakers, and human-AI interaction patterns collectively constitute reasonable care. The article cites several regulatory actions where firms were cited not for specific algorithm actions but for inadequate oversight infrastructure. For financial services AI more broadly, this implies that the design of the human-AI interaction architecture — not just human review of AI outputs — is the primary unit of legal scrutiny.
relevance: Financial services-specific application of the negligence standard — most directly relevant

title: AI Trust and Human Control: Epistemic and Practical Dimensions
analysis: This synthesis draws from multiple academic sources on the relationship between AI trust and human control. The key tension it identifies is between 'epistemic trust' — trusting AI as a source of knowledge — and 'practical trust' — relying on AI to perform actions in the world. The paper argues that these two modes of trust have different cognitive and institutional structures, and that conflating them leads to governance failures. Epistemic trust requires institutions that can validate knowledge claims; practical trust requires institutions that can attribute agency and consequence. The synthesis identifies a spectrum from human-controlled AI (human makes decisions, AI assists) through human-supervised AI (AI decides but human can override) to autonomous AI (AI decides and acts without human involvement). Critically, the paper argues that most regulatory frameworks treat these as a single dimension, leading to blunt instruments that fail to distinguish between different trust modes. The practical implication is that governance frameworks should explicitly categorise which trust mode applies to each AI use case, and apply different oversight structures accordingly.
relevance: Framework for distinguishing types of trust — foundational conceptual clarification

title: Redefining Agency: Human-AI Collaboration in Financial Services
analysis: This paper examines how agency — the capacity to make consequential decisions and bear responsibility for them — is being redistributed between humans and AI systems in financial services. It argues that the traditional model of human agency, where a named individual is legally and ethically responsible for each decision, is being strained by AI systems that make micro-decisions at scale. The paper introduces the concept of 'distributed agency' — decision-making spread across human-AI networks in ways that make traditional attribution difficult. Critically, it examines how financial institutions are responding: some are creating new roles (AI oversight officers, model risk managers) as repositories of residual human agency; others are attempting to contractually assign agency to AI systems; still others are redesigning processes to concentrate human agency at architecturally critical nodes. The paper concludes that the redistribution of agency is not a technical problem but an institutional design problem, and that financial institutions that solve it will have a significant competitive and regulatory advantage.
relevance: Directly addresses how trust and accountability structures are being redesigned in practice

title: DBS Bank AI Governance: Harvard Business School Case Study
analysis: DBS Bank's AI governance implementation represents one of the most thoroughly documented success cases in financial services AI governance. Harvard Business School researchers examined DBS's approach to AI adoption and found that the bank had achieved measurable improvements in both AI performance and governance credibility. Key elements of DBS's approach include: (1) a centralised AI governance function with clear accountability for model risk; (2) tiered AI use case assessment that maps risk to oversight intensity; (3) mandatory human-AI interaction design review as part of model approval; (4) active post-market monitoring with defined performance thresholds and escalation triggers; (5) executive-level AI ethics committee with real authority. The Harvard analysis notes that DBS treated AI governance not as a compliance exercise but as a source of competitive advantage — better governance enabled bolder AI adoption because the bank had confidence in its oversight infrastructure. The measurable outcomes included improved model performance, reduced regulatory findings, and faster AI deployment cycles due to reduced uncertainty.
relevance: Proves that mature AI governance enables rather than impedes AI adoption — the efficiency-through-trust argument

title: NatWest AI Governance: Democratising AI with Human-Intensive Design
analysis: NatWest Group's approach to AI governance is distinctive for its emphasis on workforce-wide AI literacy as a governance mechanism. Rather than concentrating AI oversight in specialist teams, NatWest invested heavily in making every business user an informed participant in AI governance — people who could understand what AI was doing, recognise when it was behaving unexpectedly, and escalate appropriately. The bank implemented a 'human-intensive AI design' philosophy where every AI tool is evaluated not just for its technical performance but for how it changes human decision-making workflows, and whether humans remain appropriately situated in consequential decisions. NatWest's approach reflects the S-TIAS principle that human oversight is most effective when it leverages human cognitive capabilities rather than requiring humans to perform beyond their capacity. The governance innovation here is structural: rather than asking 'is there a human reviewing this AI?' it asks 'is the human oversight mechanism actually cognitively accessible to the person doing the overseeing?'
relevance: Demonstrates that governance scales through workforce AI literacy, not just specialist oversight

title: Commonwealth Bank of Australia Enterprise AI Governance Framework
analysis: CBA's formalised enterprise AI governance framework represents the most mature Australian example of financial services AI governance. The framework explicitly addresses the provenance-of-trust question by establishing clear provenance chains from data inputs through model outputs to business decisions. Key structural features include: (1) a Model Inventory with documented provenance for every AI model in production; (2) a tiered risk classification that determines oversight intensity; (3) mandatory 'explainability requirements' that scale with decision consequence; (4) continuous monitoring with automated drift detection and human escalation triggers; (5) regular re-certification cycles that acknowledge trust degrades over time. The framework explicitly incorporates the principle that AI trust is not binary — models have defined confidence domains and operating ranges, and governance ensures models operate within those ranges. The Singapore MAS has cited CBA's framework as an example of effective practice, making it a de facto reference model for the region.
relevance: Australian reference case — directly applicable to local regulatory context (ASIC, APRA)

title: Protecting Regulated Data from AI Model Drift Failures
analysis: This practitioner analysis addresses the concrete problem of AI trust degradation through model drift. It catalogues three distinct drift mechanisms: (1) data drift — changes in input data distribution that alter model behaviour; (2) concept drift — changes in the relationship between inputs and outputs as real-world conditions change; (3) covariate drift — changes in the broader contextual features that affect model validity. The paper argues that each drift type requires different monitoring and mitigation approaches, and that point-in-time certifications are structurally inadequate for detecting drift-induced trust degradation. The key contribution is a practitioner's taxonomy of drift detection methods and their relative effectiveness. Critically, the paper notes that drift is not always detectable through performance metrics alone — sometimes models degrade in ways that make them confidently wrong, which is more dangerous than obviously degraded performance. The paper advocates for continuous monitoring infrastructure as a non-negotiable component of any AI trust framework, not an optional enhancement.
relevance: Makes concrete why point-in-time certification is inadequate — essential technical complement to the philosophical argument

title: Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) Agentic AI for High-Stakes Oversight 2026
analysis: This practitioner piece directly addresses the tension Ray identified: the infeasibility of humans reviewing every AI action in agentic systems. It proposes a resolution-matched supervision model where the scope and intensity of human oversight is calibrated to the consequence profile of AI actions, not the volume. The key operational insight is that agentic AI generates too many actions for human review, but the distribution of those actions' consequences is highly skewed — most actions are low-consequence, a small fraction are medium-consequence, and a tiny fraction are high-consequence. Meaningful HITL in this context means: (1) ensuring the high-consequence actions are always subject to human review or pre-approval; (2) having automated alerting for medium-consequence exceptions; (3) accepting that low-consequence actions will be taken by AI without human review, with appropriate monitoring. The article introduces the concept of 'resolution bandwidth' — the idea that human oversight infrastructure has limited capacity, and that capacity must be allocated to the highest-resolution decisions. This is a fundamentally different model of HITL than the traditional 'human reviews everything' standard.
relevance: Directly answers Ray's second research question — operationalised, not theoretical

title: AI Epistemic Trust: How AI Reconfigures Truth, Authority, and Verification
analysis: This philosophical analysis examines how AI systems are fundamentally altering the social structure of knowledge — who can claim to know things, how knowledge claims are verified, and what counts as legitimate authority. The paper argues that AI trust is not merely a technical reliability question but an epistemic infrastructure question: AI systems are becoming primary knowledge institutions, and the trust structures we build around them are building new epistemic architectures. The paper identifies three fundamental shifts: (1) from individual to collective epistemic agents — AI knowledge is inherently distributive; (2) from human-legible to machine-legible verification — AI reasoning can exceed human comprehension; (3) from institutional to infrastructural authority — AI systems are becoming the substrate through which other institutions validate knowledge claims. The implications for trust are profound: if AI is epistemic infrastructure rather than just a tool, then trust failures in AI have cascading epistemic consequences that go far beyond the immediate application. This reframes AI trust as public infrastructure concern, not just an organisational risk management question.
relevance: Highest-order philosophical framing — situates AI trust as a civilisational infrastructure question
